data.title: RB Global (RBA) Margin Slippage Tests Bullish Multi Year Earnings Growth Narrative
data.description:
RB Global (RBA) has wrapped up FY 2025 with fourth quarter revenue of US$1.2 billion and basic EPS of US$0.53, alongside net income excluding extra items of US$99.1 million. This sets a clear marker for how the year finished. Across recent periods, the company has seen quarterly revenue range from US$981.8 million in Q3 2024 to US$1.2 billion in Q4 2025, while basic EPS moved between US$0.36 and US$0.56 over that span. This gives investors a concrete view of how the top and bottom lines have tracked into the latest print. With trailing twelve month net profit margins easing slightly from 8.7% to 8.3%, this set of results puts the focus on how efficiently the business is converting that revenue base into profit.

See our full analysis for RB Global.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up with the stories investors usually tell about RB Global, and where the latest figures might support or challenge those narratives.

See what the community is saying about RB Global

Advertisement

TTM profit growth vs slowing one year pace

  • Over the last twelve months, net income excluding extra items was US$382.2 million on US$4.6b of revenue, while one year earnings growth cited in the analysis is 2.5% compared with a 5 year earnings growth rate of 16.8% per year.
  • Analysts with a bullish view point to that 16.8% multi year growth and forecast around 21.4% annual earnings growth. However, the more modest 2.5% one year earnings growth and TTM net margin of 8.3% create a tension between the longer trend and the most recent pace.
    • Bulls argue that technology upgrades, global expansion and acquisitions like Boom & Bucket and J.M. Wood can drive higher transaction volumes and recurring service revenue. This lines up with the TTM revenue base of US$4.6b, which is larger than the US$4.3b to US$4.4b levels cited a year earlier in the data.
    • At the same time, the recent margin reading of 8.3% compared with 8.7% a year earlier means the latest profitability snapshot is a bit softer than the long run earnings growth figures that bullish investors focus on.

Some bulls see the recent 2.5% one year earnings growth as a pause in a longer multi year story, while the numbers here lay out exactly how much of that story currently shows up in reported profit. 🐂 RB Global Bull Case

Premium 52.3x P/E and 45% DCF gap

  • The shares trade on a 52.3x P/E compared with a peer average of 32.1x and industry level of 25.1x. The US$107.47 share price sits well below the US$196.51 DCF fair value estimate, which implies roughly a 45% gap to that modelled fair value.
  • Critics highlight that a P/E this far above peers can be hard to reconcile with revenue that is forecast to grow 7.8% per year versus a 10.3% US market forecast, even if some models and price targets see room for upside from the current US$107.47 price.
    • Bears argue that competition from digital first platforms and peer to peer marketplaces could pressure fees, which would matter for a business currently earning an 8.3% net margin on US$4.6b of TTM revenue.
    • On the other hand, analysts in the data are cited as expecting around 21.4% annual earnings growth, which helps explain why some investors might accept a 52.3x P/E despite the share price sitting well below the US$196.51 DCF fair value and below the allowed US$129.40 analyst target reference.

The mix of a premium 52.3x P/E, an 8.3% TTM net margin and a share price near US$107 that is below both peers’ multiples and an internal DCF fair value is exactly what bearish investors are watching. 🐻 RB Global Bear Case

Margins at 8.3% with revenue growth forecasts

  • Net profit margin over the last twelve months is 8.3% on US$4.6b of revenue, slightly below the prior year’s 8.7%. Revenue is forecast to grow about 7.8% per year, below the 10.3% annual growth forecast for the wider US market.
  • The consensus style narrative that focuses on global expansion and more value added services suggests scope for higher quality earnings, and the current margin and growth forecasts provide a concrete way to judge that view.
    • Supporters of the consensus case point to acquisitions such as IAA and J.M. Wood and the build out in markets like Australia as potential drivers for increasing higher margin service revenue on top of the existing US$4.6b TTM revenue base.
    • At the same time, the margin slip from 8.7% to 8.3% and revenue growth expected to trail the wider US market mean any improvement in earnings quality would need to come from mix and efficiency rather than simply growing faster than peers.

data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider Callaway Golf (CALY) After An 83% One Year Surge?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Callaway Golf at around US$13.49 still offers value after a strong run, or if the easier gains may already have passed, this article is for you.
  • The stock has seen a 10.4% decline over the past week and a 10.9% decline over the past month. It still sits on a 15.1% gain year to date and an 83.5% return over the last year, while the 3 year and 5 year returns are 42.6% and 51.5% declines respectively.
  • Recent price swings have kept Callaway Golf on the radar of investors who follow consumer brands, with sentiment shifting between optimism about the business and caution after periods of weaker long term returns. Longer term holders are likely weighing the strong 1 year return against the more challenging 3 year and 5 year performance when considering value today.
  • Our valuation model currently gives Callaway Golf a score of 0 out of 6. In the next sections we explain what that means across different valuation methods, before finishing with a broader way to think about value that goes beyond the usual ratios.

Callaway Golf scores just 0/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Callaway Golf Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a business might be worth today by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to a present value.

For Callaway Golf, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of $137.22 million. Looking ahead, analyst inputs and extrapolated estimates point to free cash flow of $110.25 million in 2026 and $116.80 million in 2027, rising to a projected $159.01 million in 2035 according to the Simply Wall St model. All of these figures are in US$.

When these projected cash flows are discounted back, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $8.90 per share. Compared with the current share price around $13.49, this indicates the stock is about 51.6% above this DCF estimate of intrinsic value.

Result: OVERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Callaway Golf may be overvalued by 51.6%. Discover 53 high quality undervalued stocks or create your own screener to find better value opportunities.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Callaway Golf.

Approach 2: Callaway Golf Price vs Earnings

The P/E ratio is a useful yardstick for profitable companies because it links what you pay for each share to the earnings that business is currently generating. It gives you a quick sense of how many dollars investors are willing to pay for one dollar of earnings.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on two big drivers: how quickly earnings are expected to grow and how risky those earnings are perceived to be. Higher expected growth and lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while slower or more uncertain earnings usually line up with a lower multiple.

Callaway Golf currently trades on a P/E of 63.93x, compared with a Leisure industry average of 22.24x and a peer group average of 30.23x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Callaway Golf is 29.80x, which is its proprietary view of what a more fitting P/E might be once you factor in elements like earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and specific risks. This Fair Ratio can be more informative than a simple peer or industry comparison because it is tailored to the company’s own profile.

With the actual P/E of 63.93x sitting well above the Fair Ratio of 29.80x, the shares screen as expensive on this measure.

Result: OVERVALUED

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so let us introduce you to Narratives, a simple tool on Simply Wall St’s Community page where you connect your view of Callaway Golf’s story to explicit forecasts for revenue, earnings and margins. This then produces a fair value you can weigh against today’s price.

Each Narrative is your own storyline behind the numbers. It links what you think will happen with Topgolf integration, venue sales, product launches and balance sheet moves to a financial model and fair value, and then updates that view automatically when new earnings, guidance or news arrives.

For Callaway Golf right now, one Narrative might lean closer to the lower fair value views around US$10.00 that focus on risks from venue economics or changing leisure habits. Another might sit nearer the higher fair value views around US$19.00 that focus on the pure play golf identity and Topgolf stake sale. Seeing those side by side can help you decide whether the current US$13.49 share price looks above, below, or roughly in line with the story you believe.

For Callaway Golf however we will make it really easy for you with previews of two leading Callaway Golf Narratives:

  • Views Topgolf integration, venue expansion and new golf products as key drivers that can lift traffic, support higher spend per visit and strengthen Callaway Golf’s brand over time.
  • Assumes balance sheet flexibility improves through actions such as past asset sales and cost measures, which are expected to support margins, cash flow and reinvestment in higher return projects.
  • Accepts meaningful risks around discounting, tariffs, regional softness and execution, but sees them as manageable if Callaway Golf can keep growing participation in golf and experiential leisure.

data.title: McDonald's Tests GLP-1 Friendly Menu While Deepening Cultural Relevance
data.description:
• McDonald's (NYSE:MCD) is reworking its menu to appeal to Americans using GLP-1 weight loss drugs, including tests of higher protein options.
  • The company is also rolling out fresh marketing tie ups, such as the Drake OVO Afters Meal in Canada, to keep its brand culturally visible.

For a company built on burgers, fries and soft drinks, McDonald's sits at the center of changing food habits and health trends. GLP-1 medications are starting to influence how some consumers think about portions, satiety and protein intake, which is pushing quick service brands to rethink product mix. By testing new offerings, McDonald's is trying to keep its core menu relevant while responding to those evolving preferences.

At the same time, collaborations like the OVO x Drake meal show how McDonald's continues to lean on pop culture to keep traffic coming through the door. For investors, the combination of menu experiments and brand partnerships is worth tracking, because it can affect customer loyalty, visit frequency and how NYSE:MCD is perceived against other global restaurant chains over time.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for McDonald's by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on McDonald's.

We've flagged 1 risk for McDonald's. See which could impact your investment.

For McDonald’s, leaning into GLP-1 driven eating habits looks like an attempt to future proof its core business rather than overhaul it. Management has said it has not yet seen a material impact from these drugs on traffic, but testing high protein items and smaller portion formats gives the company a way to keep frequent users of GLP-1 medications in the system, instead of ceding them to brands like Chipotle or Starbucks that already market protein forward or lighter options. Combined with recent Q4 2025 momentum and a Value Leadership focus, this supports a menu mix that tries to cover both value seekers and health conscious customers.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The McDonald's Narrative

  • The push into protein focused items and new beverages ties directly into the narrative’s emphasis on menu change and digital led ordering as drivers of guest counts and long term revenue growth.
  • If GLP-1 users reduce visit frequency or spending more sharply than expected, it could challenge assumptions in the narrative about steady traffic and the payoff from large technology and store expansion plans.
  • The GLP-1 trend, and McDonald’s response to it, is not explicitly called out in the narrative, so the potential shift in portion sizes, snacking and sugary drinks may not yet be reflected in long term margin and mix expectations.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for McDonald's to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Execution risk if high protein and GLP-1 focused items add menu complexity, slow service, or increase food costs without clear support for restaurant level profitability.
  • ⚠️ Consumer tastes could shift faster than expected, leaving McDonald’s chasing trends while rivals like Burger King and Wendy’s compete aggressively on value, promotions and new formats.
  • 🎁 GLP-1 oriented products, if successful, may help McDonald’s retain visits from health conscious customers and support the brand’s relevance as eating habits evolve.
  • 🎁 High profile collaborations such as the Drake OVO Afters Meal can reinforce cultural visibility, which has recently been linked to strong marketing driven sales performance.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, it is worth watching how quickly McDonald’s moves GLP-1 oriented tests into permanent menu slots, what price points it chooses for those items, and whether management links any future same store sales trends to this customer group. Pay attention to how often celebrity or entertainment tie ins are mentioned on earnings calls alongside sales performance, since that gives you a sense of how much traffic is being driven by these campaigns. You can also track how competitors such as Yum Brands or Restaurant Brands International respond, which will hint at whether GLP-1 focused offerings become a standard category across quick service or remain more of a McDonald’s led experiment.

Bausch + Lomb (NYSE:BLCO) has put out its latest FY 2025 numbers with Q3 revenue of US$1.3b and a basic EPS loss of US$0.08, as investors weigh how far the business has come toward closing the profitability gap. The company has seen quarterly revenue move from US$1.1b in Q1 2025 to US$1.3b in Q3, while basic EPS losses over that stretch ranged from US$0.60 in Q1 to US$0.18 in Q2 and US$0.08 in Q3. This sets up the current update as a check-in on how quickly margins can repair from here.

See our full analysis for Bausch + Lomb.

With the latest figures on the table, the next step is to see how this margin story lines up with the prevailing narratives around Bausch + Lomb and where the fresh numbers push back on those views.

See what the community is saying about Bausch + Lomb

Advertisement

Losses Shrink From US$212m To US$28m

  • Net income loss narrowed from US$212 million in Q1 2025 to US$62 million in Q2 and US$28 million in Q3, while revenue over the same period sat between US$1.1b and US$1.3b.
  • Bulls point to this pattern of smaller quarterly losses as early support for their view that margins can move from about a 6.1% loss today toward a 4.0% profit margin over the next few years. However, the trailing 12 month loss of US$305 million still lines up with the cautious view that profitability is not in place today.
    • Supporters of the bullish case highlight the Q1 to Q3 swing in basic EPS from a loss of US$0.60 to a loss of US$0.08 as a sign that the business can move toward the US$242.7 million earnings level they reference for 2028.
    • At the same time, critics within the bearish narrative point out that on a trailing 12 month basis EPS remains a loss of US$0.86 and net income is still a loss of US$305 million. They see this as consistent with their focus on a multi year loss record.
If you want to see how optimistic investors are connecting this loss reduction to their long term story for the business, check out the detailed bull case for Bausch + Lomb here: 🐂 Bausch + Lomb Bull Case

Revenue Near US$5.0b, Profit Still Negative

  • On a trailing 12 month view, revenue sits at about US$5.0b against a net income loss of US$305 million and basic EPS loss of US$0.86.
  • Bears argue that this combination of sizeable revenue and continuing losses backs their thesis that the business model still has work to do, even though they also assume profit margins can move from a 6.1% loss to a 4.3% profit margin and earnings of US$247 million by 2029.
    • That cautious stance leans on the fact that the trailing 12 month loss of US$305 million follows several years where losses reportedly widened at an average rate of 53.3% a year.
    • What complicates the bearish view is that both bearish and bullish narratives are using similar earnings end points, around US$240 million to US$250 million. The main difference is how investors judge the current loss profile rather than the long term earnings number itself.
If you are wondering how skeptics interpret these US$5.0b of sales against ongoing losses, the full bear case lays out their argument in detail: 🐻 Bausch + Lomb Bear Case

P/S Of 1.3x Versus DCF Value

  • With a share price of US$17.67 and a P/S of 1.3x versus peer and industry averages of 2.6x and 2.8x, the shares are also being compared to a DCF fair value of about US$34.23, which implies a large gap between price and that model output.
  • Consensus narrative watchers highlight a tension here, because analysts reference revenue growth forecasts of around 5.7% a year and margin improvement toward 4.3%, while the current share price of US$17.67 sits close to an analyst price target of about US$17.64, even though the DCF fair value in the data is almost double that.
    • On one side, the lower P/S ratio against peers and the DCF fair value of US$34.23 are used as evidence by those who see valuation support while accepting that the business was loss making over the past year.
    • On the other, the relatively small gap between US$17.67 and the US$17.64 analyst price target suggests analysts in the supplied data are treating the stock as roughly fairly priced despite the DCF gap. Readers may want to compare this with their own expectations for revenue growth and margin repair.

Next Steps

To see how these results tie into long-term growth, risks, and valuation, check out the full range of community narratives for Bausch + Lomb on Simply Wall St. Add the company to your watchlist or portfolio so you'll be alerted when the story evolves.

After all this, do you think the tone tilts more cautious or optimistic, and what does that mean for you right now? To pressure test the upside that others see, it can help to review the rewards in detail for yourself with 3 key rewards.

See What Else Is Out There

data.title: ARMOUR Residential REIT Profit Return And Portfolio Growth Put Dividend In Focus
data.description:
• ARMOUR Residential REIT (NYSE:ARR) reported a year over year move from a loss to profitability.
  • The company recorded substantial net income for both the fourth quarter and full year.
  • ARR expanded its investment portfolio by about 60% after deploying newly raised capital.

ARMOUR Residential REIT focuses on mortgage related investments, so its earnings profile can be sensitive to interest rates, funding costs and portfolio positioning. A shift from a prior year loss to generating net income, together with a larger portfolio, gives you fresh information about how the business model is currently translating into results. For investors who mainly follow dividend headlines, this update adds more depth on how the underlying portfolio is being managed.

From here, the combination of a larger investment base and a return to profitability creates a different starting point for assessing ARR than a year ago. As new financial data and portfolio disclosures come out, you will be able to see whether this recent progress turns into a more stable pattern or remains specific to this reporting period.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for ARMOUR Residential REIT by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on ARMOUR Residential REIT.

Is ARMOUR Residential REIT's dividend sustainable? Check out what every dividend investor needs to know in our dividend analysis.

For income focused investors, the key detail in this update is that ARR is pairing a swing to profitability with a continued monthly cash dividend of US$0.24 per common share for March 2026. As a REIT, ARR is required to distribute substantially all of its ordinary taxable income, but the board still has room to adjust the payout based on earnings, liquidity, capital needs, and market conditions. The decision to keep the dividend at this level after reporting US$211.7m of Q4 net income and a full year profit signals that management currently sees capacity to support ongoing distributions from the enlarged mortgage backed securities portfolio. At the same time, Simply Wall St’s risk checks indicate that the dividend has not been well covered by earnings or free cash flow and that shareholders have experienced substantial dilution. That combination means you may want to look beyond the headline yield and focus on how recurring earnings, funding costs, and prepayment trends line up with the current payout over coming quarters.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The ARMOUR Residential REIT Narrative

  • The move back to profit and continued monthly dividend fit with the narrative of ARR using access to equity capital to scale its Agency MBS portfolio and generate income for shareholders.
  • The flagged risks around high leverage, potential funding stresses, and dividend coverage could challenge the idea of more stable distributable earnings if market conditions or interest rate trends become less favorable.
  • The narrative discusses capital raising as a way to grow earning assets, but this dividend update also highlights dilution and coverage concerns, which may not be fully captured in a growth focused story.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for ARMOUR Residential REIT to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Dividend of 16.29% is not well covered by earnings or free cash flows, which can increase the chance of future payout changes if conditions weaken.
  • ⚠️ Debt is not well covered by operating cash flow and high leverage means funding market stress or hedging issues could affect both book value and dividend capacity.
  • 🎁 Earnings are forecast to grow, which, if realized, could improve dividend coverage and support income for shareholders over time.
  • 🎁 A return to profitability alongside a larger portfolio gives management more room to align asset yields, hedges, and funding costs with its income focused strategy.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, you will want to track how ARR’s recurring earnings and cash flows compare with the US$0.24 monthly dividend, especially now that the portfolio has grown by about 60%. Pay attention to commentary from the Q4 2025 earnings call on funding costs, prepayment speeds, and leverage, as these drive how sustainable the payout looks. Changes in share count, capital raises, or buybacks will also matter, given the recent dilution flagged in the risk checks. If dividend coverage improves on a consistent basis while credit and interest rate risks stay contained, that would point to a more durable income profile than a single quarter suggests.

data.title: HF Sinclair (DINO) Q3 EPS Rebound Tests Bearish Volatility Narrative
data.description:
HF Sinclair (DINO) has just posted its FY 2025 third quarter numbers, with revenue of US$7.3b and basic EPS of US$2.16, set against trailing 12 month earnings growth of 18.7%. The company has seen revenue move from US$7.2b in Q3 2024 to US$7.3b in Q3 2025, while quarterly EPS shifted from a loss of US$0.40 to EPS of US$2.16. The trailing net profit margin sits at 1.5% versus 1.1% last year, giving investors a clearer view of how the earnings profile is shaping up. Taken together, these results point to margins that are still relatively thin but moving in a direction that keeps profitability firmly in focus for shareholders.

See our full analysis for HF Sinclair.

With the headline figures on the table, the next step is to see how these results line up with the widely held narratives around HF Sinclair, highlighting where the numbers support the story and where they start to push back against it.

See what the community is saying about HF Sinclair

Advertisement

Profit swings show how cyclical refining can be

  • HF Sinclair moved from a net loss of US$213.3 million in Q4 2024 and US$3 million in Q1 2025 to net income of US$207.0 million in Q2 2025 and US$403.0 million in Q3 2025, highlighting how quickly profitability can shift within a few quarters.
  • Bears focus on that earlier loss making stretch and argue that refining earnings can remain volatile, yet the recent US$403.0 million profit tests the bearish view that pressure on margins will necessarily translate into persistently weak results.
    • The bearish narrative points to a first quarter net loss and pressure on refining EBITDA, which is consistent with the US$3 million loss in Q1 2025, but the swing to over US$200 million and then over US$400 million of profit suggests that segment performance can rebound when conditions are more favorable.
    • For a cautious investor, the pattern here is not one way traffic; it is a reminder that the same business model that produced losses in late 2024 and early 2025 is also capable of delivering solid profits when margins and volumes support it.

When you see profit move from losses to over US$400 million in a few quarters, it underlines how quickly the story can change in this kind of business, which is exactly what critics of the sector worry about.

🐻 HF Sinclair Bear Case

TTM earnings growth and 1.5% margin back the bullish case

  • Over the last twelve months HF Sinclair earned US$391.0 million of net income on US$26.9b of revenue, giving a 1.5% net margin and 18.7% earnings growth compared with the prior year, which is well above the roughly 2% per year growth rate over the past five years.
  • Bulls argue that tighter supply and resilient fuel demand support stronger and more durable profitability, and the combination of a 1.5% trailing margin and 18.7% earnings growth gives some numerical backing to that optimism, even though margins are still thin.
    • Supporters highlight that earnings are flagged as high quality, so the US$391.0 million of trailing profit is not just an accounting quirk but reflects underlying operations, which fits the bullish story about improved utilization, efficiency projects, and renewables starting to contribute.
    • At the same time, the margin is still only 1.5% on US$26.9b of sales, so while that aligns with the bullish claim of better profitability than in the past few years, it also reminds you that small changes in refining spreads can have an outsized impact on the bottom line.

Putting those trailing numbers next to the bullish narrative helps you see why some investors think HF Sinclair is on a better footing, but also why even small margin shifts can matter a lot here.

🐂 HF Sinclair Bull Case

Valuation signals pull in different directions

  • HF Sinclair trades at US$51.57 per share with a trailing P/E of 24.3x, compared with 14.5x for the wider US Oil & Gas industry and 19x for peers, while a DCF fair value of US$204.63 points to a very large gap between that model and the current price.
  • What stands out is the tension between a premium P/E multiple and that high DCF fair value, which both bulls and bears use differently, and the numbers give you a way to test each side rather than taking either at face value.
    • Supportive of the bullish angle, the combination of 18.7% trailing earnings growth and a current price far below the US$204.63 DCF fair value is exactly the sort of setup bulls point to when they say the market may not be paying fully for HF Sinclair’s cash flow potential.
    • In line with the cautious view, the 24.3x trailing P/E versus 14.5x for the industry and 19x for peers suggests the market is already assigning HF Sinclair a richer multiple than many competitors, which bears see as leaving less room for error, especially with a dividend yield of 3.88% that is not strongly covered by earnings.

    Next Steps

    To see how these results tie into long-term growth, risks, and valuation, check out the full range of community narratives for HF Sinclair on Simply Wall St. Add the company to your watchlist or portfolio so you'll be alerted when the story evolves.

    After all this, are you feeling more bullish or cautious about HF Sinclair? If you want to move quickly and base your view on the underlying data, take a closer look at the balance of 2 key rewards and 1 important warning sign before you decide where you stand.

    See What Else Is Out There

    HF Sinclair’s thin 1.5% net margin, prior loss making quarters, and premium 24.3x P/E versus peers highlight earnings volatility that can unsettle long term holders.

    If that kind of profit swing keeps you on edge, take a few minutes to scan 80 resilient stocks with low risk scores and focus on businesses with steadier fundamentals and less earnings whiplash.

TTM profit of $27 million after prior losses

  • On a trailing twelve month basis, Quad/Graphics moved from a loss of $50.9 million at the end of Q4 2024 to net income of $27 million by Q4 2025. Over the same period, TTM EPS shifted from a loss of $1.07 per share to earnings of $0.57 per share.
  • What stands out against the bullish view that earnings quality is improving is the mix of factors behind this swing. The data cites both multi year earnings growth of 13.4% a year and a $21.8 million one off loss in the last twelve months, so investors need to separate:
    • The core pattern of quarterly net income turning positive in 2025, from $5.8 million in Q1 to $11.1 million in Q4, which supports the idea of a more profitable business model.
    • The impact of that one off $21.8 million charge in the TTM figures, which affects how clean the earnings trend really is when you apply the bullish margin expansion story.

Bulls argue that this move into positive TTM earnings could be the foundation for a stronger long term profit story, and you can see how that thinking lines up with the detailed narrative here: 🐂 Quad/Graphics Bull Case

P/E of 12.5x versus higher industry multiples

  • The company is trading on a trailing P/E of 12.5x, compared with 25.1x for the US Commercial Services industry and 31.6x for the peer average. A DCF fair value of US$16.33 sits well above the current share price of US$6.63.
  • Analysts' consensus narrative sees Quad/Graphics using AI driven automation and integrated marketing to grow higher margin services, and the current numbers partly support that value angle yet still leave questions, because:
    • The apparent gap between the share price of US$6.63, the DCF fair value of US$16.33 and the analyst target of US$9.10 points to the market pricing the stock below both modelled cash flow value and the consensus price target.
    • At the same time, TTM revenue of about US$2.4b at Q4 2025 compares with US$2.7b at Q3 2024, so the business is working off a lower sales base while trying to shift mix toward data driven marketing solutions.

Interest coverage and debt temper the bull case

  • One of the clearest risks in the data is that interest payments are not well covered by earnings, alongside a reported US$193 million of debt maturing in 2029 at a blended interest rate of 7.2% and an unstable dividend track record.
  • Bears focus on that financing pressure plus ongoing print headwinds, and the reported figures give some grounding to that cautious stance because:
    • Even with TTM net income of US$27 million by Q4 2025, the combination of interest costs and a past one off loss of US$21.8 million means cash flow has less room to absorb any setback in revenue or margins.
    • Recent revenue prints, from US$708.4 million in Q4 2024 to US$630.6 million in Q4 2025, show the business working through a smaller top line while still carrying that interest burden and credit investors' attention on leverage.

If you want to see how critics connect these balance sheet pressures to their cautious view, the full bear case lays it out in detail: 🐻 Quad/Graphics Bear Case
